stories.title: The Widow's Second Shadow
stories.text:
Sorenna had learned to live without her shadow—the one the merchant Vex stole three winters past. But today, in the night markets of Vethrim, she watched her own darkness move without her. It pooled beneath a silk merchant's feet, whispering numbers only the dead remember. Vex's shadow. The broker who bought it had sold it onward, and onward again, until it found work as a ledger-reader for the Dominion's auditors. Now it moved through the crowd with autonomous hunger, cataloguing refugees by the scent of their fear. Sorenna cornered it in an alley behind the spice quarter. "I know what you are," she said. The shadow didn't flee. It coalesced—a dark mirror of Vex's smirk. "Your widow's compass draws it to you. "Vex is unraveling. Three buyers claim ownership now. One is using me to hunt her down."

"Where?"

"The Wailing Scar. She's trading herself piecemeal to survive the Remaking. Selling memories to become nothing, so nobody can own her."

Sorenna drew the compass her grandmother had carved. Its needle spun wildly—two directions at once.

stories.title: The Cartographer's Final Rebellion
stories.text:
Henryk had drawn nine maps. He sat in the hidden archive north of Pelimor, his blinded eyes useless but his hands still remembering the ley-lines. The ninth map—the blank one—lay before him. Every time he touched pen to parchment, the world trembled. The Counter-Archive's scribes held their breath. They all knew: one more map would either break the cycle of Ascensions forever or trigger the Tenth, bringing ruin. A messenger arrived, bearing a Dominion seal and a copy of Henryk's own cartography, redrawn in phantom-ink. "You are required to complete the chart," it read. "All nine iterations await reconciliation."

Henryk laughed—bitter, cracked. "They think the map is destiny. They don't understand. I've been *preventing* the map."

He'd been blind for two years, ever since he realized that seeing the ninth chart clearly would force his hand to draw it. His students had learned to guide him by voice and touch alone. The Counter-Archive had protected him because his refusal was itself a kind of power. But refusal has a limit.

stories.title: The Tide-Thief's Mercy
stories.text:
Nara had stolen tide-magic once, and the Merfolk Keeper of Pearls had hunted her across three seasons for it. Now, standing on the rain-slick dock of Verdant Strand, she felt the Keeper rising from the deep. The creature surfaced not as a monster but as a woman—scaled, luminous, ancient. "You took what was not yours."

"I did," Nara said. No point in lies. The tide was already singing her position to every deepkin within a hundred leagues. "I had a drowning daughter. One tide-word would have saved her."

The Keeper's expression didn't soften. But she rose fully onto the dock, water running like tears from her shoulders. "The tide-magic cannot save. It only shifts currents. Your daughter would have been swept to the next realm instead of this one. The magic chooses its own destinations."

Nara had stolen it anyway. Her daughter was dead either way. "The price is your own tide-touch. You will lose the ability to feel currents, to sense the deep. You become deaf to the sea."

But the Keeper waited.

stories.title: The Bone-Smith's Price
stories.text:
Korgin Ironfoot had made a thousand blades. Never one like this. It sang in the furnace—not with joy, but with the voice of something sleeping underneath creation. Korgin's hands moved by instinct older than thought, hammering the metal into a spear-point, a king's-ransom weapon. The buyer arrived alone, wrapped in furs that smelled of pine and old magic. She was fae—Korgin could tell by the way the forge-light bent around her, as if uncertain whether to touch something that existed in two seasons at once. "I need a weapon," she said, "that can kill what should not be killed. A boundary guardian. Something sworn to eternal service."

Korgin laid the spear across his anvil. The metal hummed. "That costs more than gold."

"I know." The fae woman smiled sadly. "My daughter loved a human man. Against the Laws. To unmake the marriage as if it had never been. If I kill them with this, I sever the Law itself.

stories.title: The Goblin-King's Census
stories.text:
Grethuk the Goblin-King had never wanted to rule. But when the Remaking shattered the surface world's ledgers, the Underground became valuable—and someone had to keep the counts. He sat in the Great Cavern of Blackstone, surrounded by three hundred goblins, dwarves, and odder things, all arguing about who owned which tunnel-section now that gold meant nothing. The Dominion wanted his cooperation. The Counter-Archive wanted his testimony. The wyverns wanted his access to deep veins of consciousness-fragments, somehow concentrated in the stone itself. Grothuk, who'd been a locksmith before circumstance crowned him, had a simpler idea. "We close the gates," he announced. "The Underground is sealed. No trade with above. We inventory ourselves—every creature, every skill, every scrap of memory we can gather. We become *our own kingdom*."

A dwarf-elder stood. "The surface will starve us out."

"They're too busy starving themselves," Grethuk replied. "By the time they remember we exist, we'll have rebuilt. When they need us again, we'll set the price."

It was a wager. The Underground had always been liminal—a place between worlds.

stories.title: The Copper Thief's Choice
stories.text:
Marin the Copper-Handed had stolen from every merchant house in Saltmere's Quarter—but never from the Archive Tower's vault. Until hunger made her desperate. The Dominion's auditors had seized her sister's glasswork as "unlicensed consciousness-craft." Now the girl starved in a work-camp. Marin needed fragments—not gold, which was ash now—to buy her freedom. The vault lay seven floors beneath Keshtir's administrative heart. Her copper fingers, forged by a curse-merchant years ago, could pick any lock ever made. But this lock sang. She could hear it from the street: a high, harmonic frequency that made her teeth ache. An old bone-singer named Sorren found her studying the entrance. "You'll go in," Sorren said. It wasn't a question. "The auditors will hunt me."

"They hunt everyone now. But that lock—it's carved with Elder Tongue. It remembers the Remaking's first moment. What you take from it will carry that memory."

Marin descended. Her copper fingers trembled as she touched the lock. It burned—not with heat but with recognition.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Debt Collector
stories.text: Morthyx the wyvern had spent four centuries collecting debts—mostly fragments of consciousness worth nothing now. On 2026-06-24, at Pale Crossing waystation, she found her last client: a refugee woman trading memories for bread. The widow offered Morthyx something else instead: a choice. She sang an old bargain-song, the kind bone-singers taught to those fleeing the Remaking. Morthyx could either claim the fragment and add it to her worthless hoard, or help this woman reach the Counter-Archive waystation three days north, where testimony itself had become currency. For the first time, the wyvern-lord understood: mercy was the only debt that compounded into something real. She flew the widow there at dawn, and didn't charge her anything.

stories.title: The Reef-Keeper's Last Bargain
stories.text:
The Tear of the Drowned God was failing. Echo-Mereth, captain of the deepkin boundary-guard, could feel it through her bones—the seal weakening, the abyss pressing upward, reality stretching thin. Three versions of her stood in the Chamber of Council: Mereth-Echo, pragmatic and resigned; Mereth-Mirror, who wanted to break the seal entirely and begin anew; and Echo-Mereth herself, carrying the original oath. The Merfolk Keeper of Pearls emerged from the abyssal depths, ancient and terrible. "One captain must descend," she said. "A willing sacrifice. The Tear requires a choice made freely, not forced by covenant."

Mereth-Echo stepped forward immediately. "I will—"

"No," Mereth-Mirror interrupted. "We are three. Let all three descend together. Let the Tear have three anchors instead of one."

Echo-Mereth understood then. Her reflections were not copies. They were *choices*—the paths she might have walked. To save the boundary, she didn't need to erase them. She needed to claim them. The three captains joined hands. As they sank, Echo-Mereth felt their memories flood her—the pragmatism, the rebellion, the steadfast love.

stories.title: The Cartographer's Unfinished Map
stories.text: Aldrin was the youngest apprentice in the Counter-Archive's hidden scriptoriums. The Dominion wanted maps; the Counter-Archive wanted truth. Aldrin had been given a task neither faction fully understood: to chart the Bleached Wastes not as a place on a ledger, but as it truly existed. No one had succeeded. Mardoc, the greatest cartographer of the age, had distributed his consciousness across ley-lines trying to map nine iterations simultaneously. Most who ventured into the wastes returned broken or not at all. Aldrin decided to *not* map the place. Instead, she would listen to it. For three months, she camped at the Rust Gate's threshold. Each dawn, she walked a different path into the Bleached Wastes and stopped. She listened to the wind across ancient stones, watched how light fell on pre-human ruins, felt the ground remember what had stood there. She drew what she heard. Not walls and distances, but *songs*—curves that showed how sound traveled, spirals that marked where the dead still whispered, blank spaces where reality had worn thin and forgotten itself.

stories.title: The Smith's Final Fire
stories.text:
Torven Ironfoot had forged weapons for warlords, kings, and outcasts. Now, in the Remaking's aftermath, he forged them for hope. The Deep Forges hummed with an ancient song—not Dominion furnace-craft, but something older. Each blade Torven beat from meteoric iron sang with memory. His apprentices, a mix of refugees and rebellious glass-workers, fed consciousness-fragments into the fires. Not as currency. As prayer. A Dominion auditor arrived with a commission that made Torven's hammer-hand shake. They wanted a sword that could cut through testimony itself—a blade that would sever any spoken truth that contradicted the phantom-ledgers. "A truth-killer," the auditor said. "You'll be remembered forever."

Torven stared at the fragment-payment. Enough to feed his forges for a year. Enough to buy his apprentices' freedom from the Dominion's tax-collectors. He returned to the furnace. But instead of a truth-killer, he forged something else: a hammer. It was massive, double-headed, inscribed with Elder Tongue syllables along its haft. When Torven swung it, it didn't strike—it *sang*.
